implemented: ✅ Right to erasure (partial operational path exists): user deletion workflow removes user record plus related tokens/requests in course-forge-backend/src/main/java/com/mundrisoft/courseforge/service/UserService.java (deleteUser).

✅ Personal data collection is explicit through DTO contracts with validation annotations (e.g., UserRequestDto, UpdateBillingContactRequestDto, SetPasswordDto) under course-forge-backend/src/main/java/com/mundrisoft/courseforge/dto.
✅ Some retention logic exists for temporary/operational files and editor sessions via scheduled cleanup in FileCleanupService and SCORM session cleanup services.
partial: ⚠️ Data portability is implemented for course/package exports (course-forge-backend/src/main/java/com/mundrisoft/courseforge/export/controller/CourseExportController.java) but not clearly for subject-level personal data export.
⚠️ Data minimization is inconsistent: DTO validation exists, but broad collection of contact/profile fields is present across multiple DTOs without code-level purpose/necessity enforcement.
⚠️ Purpose limitation is partly implicit in service/controller flows, but there is no explicit code-level tagging/enforcement of processing purposes.

⚠️ Data inventory and access mapping are partially covered through DTO/entity/service usage, but there is no centralized record of what personal data is held, where stored, and who can access each category.
⚠️ Data retention exists in isolated flows (file/session cleanup, user deletion path) but not as a complete GDPR retention schedule per data category.

⚠️ Security controls are partially implemented (JWT auth, BCrypt, selective audit logging), but GDPR-specific safeguards such as pseudonymization/anonymization policy enforcement are not uniformly applied.

⚠️ Email consent-related mechanics exist in auth/verification flows, but explicit GDPR-grade marketing consent state and unsubscribe lifecycle governance are not fully modeled in current backend artifacts.
missing: ❌ Consent tracking controls are not evidenced in code (no clear consent entity/ledger/versioned consent capture flow found).
❌ Comprehensive GDPR subject-access/export endpoint for user/account/billing activity data is not evidenced.
❌ Centralized retention/deletion policy engine for persisted PII across all entities is not evidenced.
❌ No explicit minors/age-threshold (under 16) collection and parental consent control flow is evidenced in backend privacy controls.
❌ No dedicated record of processing activities (RoPA-style) is evidenced for GDPR accountability requirements.
❌ No explicit third-party processor governance register (processor list, purpose, DPA status, controls) is evidenced in code-level compliance assets.
❌ No international transfer control register (EEA to non-EEA transfer basis, safeguards, risk assessment references) is evidenced in compliance artifacts.
❌ Cookie-consent management backend support (opt-in categories, persistent opt-out, consent recall/change history) is not evidenced in current backend scope.
❌ Form-level consent metadata capture (privacy notice acceptance, correspondence opt-in choices, timestamped consent proof) is not comprehensively modeled across data collection endpoints.

❌ Breach-response readiness controls (72-hour supervisory notification workflow, affected-user notification workflow, incident evidence package) are not implemented as structured backend process modules.
❌ Privacy policy version acceptance tracking (which user accepted which policy version and when) is not evidenced as a dedicated compliance control.

implemented: ✅ Authentication token validation exists via JWT utility and interceptor (JwtUtil, JwtInterceptor, WebConfig).
✅ Password hashing with BCrypt is configured and used (SecurityConfig password encoder; usage in auth/user service flows).
✅ Audit event mechanism exists (UserAccessEvent + AuditTrailListener) and at least one controller publishes audit events (UserController).
✅ Basic health monitoring exposure exists through Actuator health endpoint in application.properties and application-prod.properties.
partial: ⚠️ Access control is split/inconsistent: Spring Security filter chains permit all (SecurityConfig), while auth enforcement is mainly custom interceptor/manual checks.
⚠️ RBAC exists but is uneven: isolated @PreAuthorize and manual role checks in controllers/services, not uniformly enforced through a centralized policy model.
⚠️ Audit logging coverage appears limited to selected actions; no repository-wide evidence of comprehensive security/audit trail for all critical data changes.
missing: ❌ Evidence of Redis caching controls is missing because Redis integration itself is not present (no Redis client/config usage found).
❌ Evidence of Riak storage controls is missing because Riak integration itself is not present (no Riak usage found).
❌ Centralized security monitoring instrumentation (e.g., broad metrics/tracing/alert hooks) is not clearly evidenced beyond health endpoint and application logs.

implemented: ✅ Password protection control is implemented with BCrypt (SecurityConfig + auth/user service encoding calls).
✅ JWT signatures are cryptographically validated using HS256 in JwtUtil.
✅ TLS usage is configured for SMTP (smtp.ssl.enabled=true, TLS protocol settings in application property files).
partial: ⚠️ Encryption practice is mixed: strong password hashing exists, but symmetric utility uses hardcoded key and AES/ECB mode in EncryptionUtil.
⚠️ Secure configuration is partial: production profile reduces verbosity and limits actuator exposure, but insecure/unsafe defaults remain in config set (e.g., DB useSSL=false, ddl-auto=update).
⚠️ Risk controls are partially documented in code comments and selective checks, but not uniformly codified across all sensitive flows.
missing: ❌ Secrets management is not implemented in a compliant code pattern: numerous secrets/credentials/API keys are directly present in application.properties and application-prod.properties.
❌ No code evidence of key rotation/revocation lifecycle for JWT secrets/API secrets.
❌ No code evidence of systematic field-level encryption policy for sensitive persisted business/PII data.

risks: Hardcoded secrets and credentials in config files (spring.datasource.password, jwt.secret, OpenAI keys, SMTP password, ZeptoMail key, Stripe keys) create credential exposure and compromise risk.
Authorization model risk: SecurityConfig permits all requests at filter-chain level, with enforcement moved to custom interceptor/manual checks, increasing bypass/misconfiguration risk.
Potential sensitive-data logging risk: request-associated values (e.g., imageUrl, sessionId, search criteria) and exception message propagation appear in controller logging/response paths.
Cryptographic weakness risk from EncryptionUtil hardcoded AES key and AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding usage.
Data subject rights coverage risk: deletion exists for user path, but comprehensive personal-data portability and consent lifecycle evidence is not present.
Assurance gap risk for cache/NoSQL controls because Redis/Riak control implementation is absent in code.
GDPR governance risk: missing centralized processor/international transfer controls can expose the organization to cross-border compliance violations.
Consent evidence risk: absence of structured cookie/form/privacy-policy consent logs may weaken legal defensibility during audits or complaints.
Incident response risk: without breach workflow orchestration and notification tracking, 72-hour GDPR notification obligations may be missed.
unknowns: Requires infrastructure or policy validation: whether repository secrets are overridden by runtime secret managers and not used in deployed environments.
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: production network controls around actuator and API endpoints (private access, WAF/API gateway rules).
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: log pipeline encryption, retention, RBAC, redaction, and SIEM forwarding controls.
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: database/storage encryption at rest, backup encryption, and KMS/HSM governance.
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: formal consent governance/process may exist outside application code (legal/policy/workflow systems).
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: incident monitoring/alerting stack may exist outside this repository.
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: whether a separate legal/compliance system maintains RoPA, processor DPAs, and transfer impact assessments outside this repository.
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: whether frontend/web platform already implements GDPR cookie banner mechanics with backend consent persistence not represented here.
Requires infrastructure or policy validation: whether formal incident response runbooks and regulatory notification workflows are implemented in SOC/ITSM tools external to codebase.
